description: This is an entry-level course where students will explore a variety of drawing and painting materials such as colored pencils, acrylic, charcoal, digital painting, etc. Students will work on developing foundational drawing skills and exploring communicating ideas through artwork. No prior experience required for this course. Come and experiment with new art making materials.

This is a great course to further sculpture learning! We will do more unusual 3D arts like stained glass, wire animals, wool felting, and polymer clay. It is NOT a hard class and is an entry level course. Students will be introduced to sculptors, both contemporary and from the past, and learn how to critically evaluate their work and the works of others in the 3-D format.

This is the class if you already know sculpture basics and you want to progress on your own in a specific area. It is an independent art course where students will have the freedom to set their own goals and work towards advanced 3-D artwork of their choice. We will be discussing portfolio building, careers in 3-D art and different media that can be used such as glass, metal, polymer clay and mixed media. Do NOT take this class without some basic 3-D art knowledge. Sophomores allowed only with permission.

Year Long Combined with 3D Graphics 3D Animation is a one semester course using 3D graphics software to produce 3D models and animations. This course will introduce students to 2D and 3D modeling, animation planning, storyboard development, and the animation process.

Year Long Combined with 3D Animation Students will use 3D graphics software to produce 3D models. This course will introduce students to 2D and 3D modeling, creation and application of textures, mapping, lighting, camera techniques, and rendering of 3-D models.

Suggested Prior Coursework: Honors Art is preferable. Students should come in with drawing and art-making skills. This year-long course is designed to prepare a student to submit an AP Studio Art portfolio. Students spend the year creating an inquiry and then investigating this idea in their artwork. Rather than other art classes where the teacher chooses the assignments, each student has the freedom to make the artwork in the medium and style they want. Because this class is focused on student choice, it is expected that students will come in with artistic experience in how to make art (whether drawing, painting, photography, etc.), composition, and coming up with ideas for their own artwork. Time in class is also used to experiment with art-making, discuss professional and student artist work, and work on the portfolio. Coming up with ideas can be challenging, but is also exciting and rewarding. This is the most advanced art class offered at THS. If making art is something you enjoy doing for fun, this class will really push you to find your own artistic style and voice and prepare you for art classes in college.

sourceTitles: AP ART HISTORY A100A3 CE (A, SS)
description: Fine Art and/or Social Studies Elective credit may be earned. This course covers the history of all parts of the world, from Europe to Africa to Asia to the Americas, through the lens of the artwork each culture left behind. This course promotes understanding and appreciation of all cultures as students learn more about the history, religions, philosophies, and ideas that shape the world we live in today. These differences are viewed through the artwork created whether buildings, sculptures, or paintings. Students learn to recognize, analyze, and appreciate the artistic skill and deeper themes that artists have communicated across time. 250 works are included in the AP Art History image set, so there is a lot of memorizing, research, and writing in this class. These become life skills that prepare students for college and, more importantly, train students to become critical thinkers as well as open-minded citizens of the world. After taking this class, you will never look at the world the same way.

sourceTitles: AP BIOLOGY CE S300A1/2 (SC1, ASC) DBIO16
description: *UVU credit may be earned (Biology 1610/1615 - 5 credit hours, college biology for majors) Recommendations: Good study and attendance habits are necessary prior to enrolling in this course. This course is taught at a college level for students who are interested in continuing their formal education after high school. The class will focus on four big ideas in Biology: 1. The process of evolution drives the diversity and unity of life, 2. Biological systems utilize free energy and molecular building blocks to grow, to reproduce and to maintain dynamic homeostasis., 3. Living systems store, retrieve, transmit and respond to information essential to life processes., 4. Biological systems interact, and these systems and their interactions possess complex properties. Students prepare to take the AP Biology exam. Concurrent enrollment through UVU is available. Science Core Chemistry

AP Calculus AB covers the topics generally found in a first semester college calculus course. Topics include limits, derivatives and their applications, integrals and their applications, as well as the calculus of trigonometric, logarithmic, and exponential functions. A heavy emphasis is placed on preparing students to take the advanced placement test in mathematics. A graphing calculator is required for this course.

AP Calculus BC covers the topics generally found in a full year college curriculum calculus course. In addition to the topics of limits, derivatives and their applications, and integrals and their applications, students will study the convergence of series, and the calculus of vectors, parametric and polar curves. A heavy emphasis is placed on preparing students to take the advanced placement test in mathematics. A graphing calculator is required for this course.

This course is taught at a college level for students interested in intensive study in chemistry. Students will investigate the nature of the atom, infer physical and chemical properties based on chemical bonding and periodic tendencies, investigate chemical reactions, and study chemical systems and the processes of chemical analysis. AP exam available. Science Core Physics

sourceTitles: AP COMPUTER SCIENCE A C6161/2 (SC, ASC)
description: Year *UVU credit may be earned AP Computer Science A emphasizes object-oriented programming methodology with an emphasis on problem solving and algorithm development and is meant to be the equivalent of a first semester undergraduate course in computer science. It also includes the study of data structures and abstraction. Note: the course will be taught in Java. Students registering for this class can opt to have a Concurrent Enrollment (CE) option to obtain UVU credit instead of AP.

sourceTitles: AP ENGLISH LANG AND COMPOSITION ENLAA1/2 (ALA)
description: Replaces English 11 Honors AP Language and Composition is an introductory, college level writing course focused on composition, rhetoric, and argument. Students learn close, critical reading and analysis and compose argument, rhetorical analysis, and synthesis essays; readings focus on both nonfiction and fiction texts. Typically, passing the AP Language and Composition exam earns three (3) credits for freshman composition (e.g., English 1010). Some colleges offer both freshman and sophomore composition, six credits (6), for passing with a high score. Check with the colleges you plan to attend for their AP credit awards policies.

AP Literature is an introductory, college level literature course focused on works of fiction, drama, and poetry from various periods and cultures. Students learn close, critical reading and analysis and compose interpretive essays. Typically, passing the AP Literature exam earns three (3) credits general education (GE) credit humanities distribution/Introduction to Literature credit. Some colleges offer this credit for both the freshman and sophomore levels, six credits (6), for passing with a high score. Check with the colleges you plan to attend for their AP credit awards policies. COLLEGE WRIT (College Writing 1010*) ENCW1/2 (ALA) *UVU credit may be earned Course Description: College Writing Concurrent Enrollment follows Utah Valley University’s (UVU) required curriculum for freshman composition and awards college credit for English 1010 upon successful completion of the course. Students will read assigned textbook chapters from UVU’s assigned text, complete chapter submissions, draft and complete multiple revisions of essays in different genres, and more. The course is both reading and writing intensive. The course instructor utilizes UVU’s CANVAS course for assignment submissions, virtual discussions, and access to lesson materials and schedule. Though a hybrid-online course, as it is simultaneously a high school course, attendance is required. (Please take a fully online, asynchronous course if in-person attendance is an issue.) Students must register with and become UVU students in order to earn the credit and to schedule mandatory conferences with UVU’s Writing Center tutors. The difference between College Writing Concurrent Enrollment at THS and English 1010 at UVU is that at UVU the course is a single semester; at THS the course is year-long (i.e. two semesters), and sufficient grades also earn four ELA quarter credits towards high school graduation as well as English 1010 credit through UVU.

AP European History is a rigorous academic course that provides a basic narrative of events and movements in European History from 1450 to the present. It is designed to prepare students for the demands of a college education by providing experience in college level reading and writing, as well as assist you in the preparation for the AP European History Test. AP European is a good class to prepare sophomores to take AP US History or as an upperclassman to enhance what was learned in US History.

This course is taught at a college pace for students interested in continuing their formal education after high school. This course will provide students with a general understanding of the governmental, political, and legal systems of the United States. Practical learning skills will be combined with the instruction from outside resource persons and varied activities which will enable students to learn about law and the legal systems of this nation.

This course is designed for highly motivated students serious about their study of music. Students will be instructed in music theory, history, sight-singing and tonal dictation. The class requires practice outside of class time as well as book study. The items taught are designed to ensure a good result on the AP Music Theory exam given at the end of the year. This is a valuable class for students interested in continuing their study of music beyond high school.

Previous physics is NOT required. This course is equivalent to the first physics class required in college for non-science majors. It fulfills pre-med requirements at many schools and helps prepare students for the MCAT. Students taking this course should have successfully completed or be concurrently enrolled in Secondary Math III. It is a practical course dealing with the principles of motion, forces, energy, momentum, rotation, oscillations, and gravitation. Emphasis is placed on problem solving using a rigorous mathematical treatment; therefore, students must have a solid background in math. Students have the option to take the Calculus-based AP Physics C: Mechanics test instead for those interested in science or engineering careers. Applied Science/Advanced Courses

This course is taught at a college level pace for students interested in continuing their formal education after high school. It is for the academically talented student desiring to earn college credit in psychology in the Advanced Placement Program. The purpose of the AP course in psychology is to introduce students to the systematic and scientific study of the behavior and mental processes of human beings and other animals. Students are exposed to the psychological facts, principles, and phenomena associated with each of the major subfields within psychology. They also learn about the methods psychologists use in their science and practice. In addition to studying psychology, students will learn and practice many of the skills that will prepare them to be successful college students.

Additional $5 Fee required This is an advanced level class designed to increase and enhance proficiency of major language skills in Spanish. Emphasis will be on improving proficiency in all language skill areas with special emphasis dedicated to improving reading and writing skills. Students should remember that this course leads to earning college credits, therefore, it will be run as such. Students are expected to be responsible for their learning and are expected to attend class on a regular basis. Class instruction will be given in Spanish. Students have the option of taking the AP Spanish Language Exam in May.

AP Statistics is an Advanced Placement course where students will learn how to collect, organize, summarize, and analyze data to gain insights into patterns and trends and to make predictions of future behavior. Probability, counting theory, simulations and mathematical modeling will be studied. Technology will be used to create simulations and analyze data. Full Year Advantage: A full year to learn deeply — compared to a single semester in college. PHYSICAL EDUCATION / HEALTH

AP US History is a college-level course designed to provide students with a factual knowledge necessary to deal critically with problems and materials in United States history. Along with learning historical content, the course will also train students to analyze and interpret primary sources, including documentary materials, maps, statistical tables, and pictorial and graphic evidence of historical events. The course moves rapidly so it is recommended that only students who read well and have a strong background in US History register for the class. Social Studies Elective

Introduces American Sign Language (ASL) to students with little or no previous experience with ASL. Course is taught with immersion and a comprehensible approach to language learning. Emphasizes basic expressive and receptive conversational skills through active student participation. Includes introduction to American Deaf culture, Deaf history, and ASL history.

Builds on experiences gained in ASL 1. Course is taught with immersion and a comprehensible approach to language learning. Emphasizes stronger expressive and receptive conversational skills through active student participation. Includes more information on Deaf culture, Deaf history, Deaf issues, and ASL history.

Students will learn advanced conversational and performance skills in this course. your fluency level in this course will increase and you will be able to converse in most real-life situations. You will continue to learn about Deaf culture and will cover a variety of topics in more depth. Evaluations will be based on performance, receptive skills, written evaluations and papers.

Students will develop skills beginning with an understanding of the basic elements and concepts, double entry accounting systems. Skills include understanding the accounting cycle; entering transactions in journals; posting to ledgers; compiling end of period worksheets; adjusting and closing entries, statements, and reports; completing banking activities and payroll systems. Students will do real life accounting simulations. The state competency test for this area will be given to students who are enrolled in this class. Passing the state test will allow students to show future employers they have a marketable skill.. If you plan on majoring in Accounting or Business Management it is strongly encouraged to take Accounting I in the fall of Junior year and Accounting 2 in the fall of your Senior year.

Students will develop advanced skills to those learned in Accounting 1. Additional concepts to be mastered include uncollectible accounts, depreciation on assets, interpretation of financial information, and notes and interest. The state competency test for this area will be given to students who are enrolled in this class. Passing the state test will allow students to show future employers they have a marketable skill. If you plan on majoring in Accounting or Business Management it is strongly encouraged to take Accounting I in the fall of Junior year and Accounting 2 in the fall of your Senior year.

Audition Audition on brass or woodwind instrument required Wind Symphony is the premier concert band at THS. Membership is by audition only, according to ability and instrumentation needs. The audition consists of 6 major scales (1 octave), and sight-reading. Students may also participate in a Spring Tour. The Wind Symphony will also perform with the Orchestra at some concerts. This band is reserved for the dedicated musicians at THS who show self-motivation and a high level of musicianship. Students in the Wind Symphony are required to attend before-school rehearsals every B-day at 7:10 am. Private instruction is highly recommended. Attendance at before, or after school sectionals, rehearsals, festivals and performances is required.

description: Audition Required This intermediate-advanced level course is a continuation of Dance 2, focusing in depth on basic principles that will improve the student’s technique, strength, flexibility, performance, stamina, and overall dance ability. The biggest focus will be on taking prior skills learned and being able to create and compose using dance elements, improvisation, choreography forms, performance skills, and artistic expression. Students will also study criticism and aesthetics as well as create and perform student choreographed works biannually.

Drawing 2 is a drawing class where we explore the drawing mediums more in depth. We will cover still life drawing, animals, portraits and landscapes. The media used is more unusual media like ink, charcoal, pastels, etc. There is more freedom in this class to explore your own style and skill level.

Do you want a class where you can work on your own stuff and style? This is it! You will set your own goals and strive towards mastery in the area you choose. We will be discussing portfolio building, careers in art and different media like charcoal, oil painting, pastels, etc. but you will get to choose where to focus your efforts. This is an EXCELLENT class to take alongside AP Art because it will give you time and help to work on your projects due in that class. Please do not take this class without a basic understanding of drawing and/or painting. Sophmores allowed only with permission.

No fee required This course introduces students to the world of commercial art and advertising design. Through hands-on projects, you will develop strong visual communication skills while creating original designs. You’ll build real-world skills used in careers such as graphic design, illustration, branding and art direction. The course covers design principles, typography, layout, color theory, and industry-standard software including Photoshop, Illustrator, and InDesign. Projects include, logo, decal, poster, t-shirt, page layout design and more. Students will also learn how professionals communicate with clients, meet deadlines, and confidently present creative concepts.

A period released from school. Student(s) may not be on school property during “released” time.EARNING UVU CREDIT WHILE AT TIMPANOGOS There are several ways Timpanogos students may earn college/university credit during high school. Some college credit may be earned without leaving the THS campus. CONCURRENT ENROLLMENT Concurrent Enrollment is a program whereby certain classes can count as college credit concurrently with their high school credit. They will receive high school credit and college credit. Not every course taught at THS is a concurrent enrollment course and not every teacher is approved to give concurrent credit so it is important to check with your counselor about which courses and teachers are available for earning college credit. This should take place in your annual CCR as you plan for your next year's classes. Simply registering for a THS concurrent enrollment class does not automatically mean you will receive UVU credit. There are specific procedures to follow. YOU AND YOUR STUDENT ARE RESPONSIBLE FOR YOUR REGISTRATION. If you are interested in taking a concurrent enrollment course via UVU you should go online to https://www.uvu.edu/concurrent/ First, click on parent/student information, then on current class lists. The classes change from time to time, but this will be the most up to date list. When you are ready to enroll you will click on “click here to enroll”. If you have never taken a concurrent enrollment course, you must first apply for admission to UVU. Timpanogos retains no record of your registration so it is important that you print a copy of your registration. There is a one time $35 enrollment fee. This must be paid online as you register for UVU. You will never have to pay this fee again. You should check with your THS teacher to make sure that you are on their UVU concurrent enrollment rolls. Some courses have certain prerequisites you must meet in order to enroll for college credit. You should never concurrently enroll in a subject which you have received previous grades of a C or below. Remember these are college level classes and your grades will follow you on a permanent college transcript. Also if you drop the class after a specified date you will receive a UW on your transcript, which is the equivalent of an F. TIMPANOGOS CONCURRENT ENROLLMENT OFFERINGS Many THS courses offer Concurrent Enrollment-CE credit. Students can earn both high school AND college credit at the same time. Students register for the course at THS with their counselor during regular registration. The student also enrolls online at UVU for the concurrent class. Information on how to apply is available from your counselor or the Concurrent Enrollment Facilitator. The class is taught by a THS teacher and it follows our school A Day/B Day schedule. At the end of the year, the grades are transferred to the university by the CE teacher, where they remain on the student’s permanent college transcript. Concurrent Enrollment Information subject to change. Please see your counselor. MOUNTAINLAND TECHNICAL COLLEGE-MTEC In partnership with Alpine School District, Mountainland Technical College (MTEC) provides high school students advanced technical training for high school credit. Credit and grades are acknowledged by the student’s school. High school students may enroll in MTEC programs tuition-free and learn marketable job skills before high school graduation. Students are responsible for applicable fees and will be required to purchase books and other supplies depending on the various program requirements. The fees vary in amount and must be paid upon enrollment. Students must be at least 16 years old and register through their high school counselor to attend MTEC. Classes are offered at various times and on several campuses. When registering, carefully read the information regarding location, day and times. Registration and cancellation should be done through the assistance of the student's high school counselor. For more information call (801) 863-6282 or visit our website www.mtec.edu THS REGISTRATION Graduation Requirement Worksheet Timpanogos High School offers an excellent variety of classes. When choosing classes we encourage students to: *Consider future plans and goals, personal interests, learning styles and preferences *Read class descriptions in this guide *Talk with parents, counselors, teachers, friends and mentors Sophomore and Junior students will discuss future classes during their scheduled individual College & Career Readiness-CCR appointment with their counselor. Invitations will be mailed to students and their parents with a scheduled time. Canceled and missed appointments may be rescheduled. Freshman will complete their CCR at the junior high.

An instructional course that describes the scientific study of the husbandry of fish populations for recreational, ecological, and commercial purposes, and the application of such studies to the management of marine life resources and fisheries. The course includes instruction in principles of aquatic and marine biology, water resources, fishing production and management operations, fishing regulations, water quality monitoring, and the management of recreational and commercial fishing activities.

Ladies' A Cappella is a women's chorus aimed at strengthening women's voices and providing opportunities for enjoying a vast selection of fantastic women's music! This Choir rehearses and performs significant women's literature from various periods and styles. Students receive instruction in vocal technique, music literacy, and rehearsal etiquette to prepare them for participation in other auditioned choirs at THS and in future college/university, community, or church choirs. Women's chorus is for singers who want to continue their involvement in choir and improve their musical abilities.

This is an entry-level art course that will explore a number of different media and projects. As the title suggests, this class gives students a chance to explore different styles, media, artists, and processes. Students will work in 2-dimensional media such as drawing and painting but also 3-dimensional media such as sculpture and glass.

This is a UVU Concurrent Enrollment course that will give an Art 1010 credit. In this class, we will learn about cultures around the world based on themes, and then spend time making artwork based on these themes. We will explore printmaking, painting, mandalas, masks, and other fun projects. We will also learn how to discuss art and art-making techniques. We will also go on a field trip to see some actual works from different cultures in person. Learn how to be a global citizen, and have fun making art and meeting friends while doing it.

This course is meant to be a deeper full-year study into the basics of art such as drawing, composition, as well as developing creativity and each student’s own artistic ideas. Students will use diverse types of media such as pencil, oil, colored pencil, pastel, watercolor, printmaking, and mosaics (the media studied changes every year). The goal for this class is to have each student leave feeling comfortable with their drawing and painting ability and for each student to be able to communicate their ideas in a clear, well-designed way. This class is geared to get sophomores and juniors ready to take AP Art so seniors are not allowed in the class without permission. This course will be taught one semester by Mrs. Forman and one semester by Ms. Davis, so you students get a chance to learn from both of these teachers.

Have you ever dreamed of creating amazing desserts or breads? In this fast paced class you will learn all about creating bakery and pastry items. This class covers baking equipment, yeast breads, pastries, fillings, cakes, and cookie production. With these skills you’ll be the favorite child, friend and neighbor. *Repeat only with Teacher Approval

Students will learn about the structure and function of cells, the patterns, processes, and the environments of living organisms needed, in order to support necessary life functions. They will explore the cause-and-effect relationships of heredity, the role of DNA in gene expression and protein synthesis, and how the mechanisms of genetic variation can lead to diversity within and among species, as well as the great diversity of species as a result of evolution by natural selection. Hands-on, student-centered activities allow students to explore the relevance of biology in their lives.

Students will learn about biotechnology applications in science, industry, medicine and forensics. Students will learn to safely work with bacteria, DNA and proteins. Skills learned in this class will have direct application for students wanting to pursue a career in any biotechnology related fields including medicine. Students enrolled in the course will be eligible for concurrent enrollment at UVU. This CE course fulfills the general life science requirement for graduation at UVU.

Concepts include overall digital experience, digital communications, digital media, presentation skills, social media, career exploration, workplace etiquette and job interviews, and creation of a personalized digital portfolio. Students will also be exposed to cloud computing and Google apps, work with digital images, videos, and audio podcasts, plan a business trip, and collaborate with team members using online tools to create, share and edit files. The goal of this class is to help students gain the knowledge and skills that will enable them to be more marketable by high school graduation.

Drama 1 is an introductory course designed to build confidence, creativity, and communication skills. Students will explore the foundations of acting, including improvisation, vocal projection, and character development. Beyond the stage, students will dive into theatre history, tracing the evolution of storytelling from Ancient Greek tragedies to the modern Broadway stage. Whether you’re an aspiring star or just looking to break out of your shell, this course provides a supportive environment to find your voice.

Ready to take your craft to the next level? Drama 2 builds upon the skills established in the introductory course, shifting focus toward technical mastery and stylistic versatility. Students will engage in intensive scene study, exploring various acting methodologies and complex script analysis. We will expand our historical knowledge by examining influential movements such as Elizabethan theatre, Realism, and Avant-Garde performance. This course emphasizes collaborative ensemble work and prepares students for more demanding roles and sophisticated production techniques.

This intermediate course is designed for students who have mastered the basics of Drama 1 and 2 and are ready to dive deeper into the professional process of creating theatre. Drama 3 serves as a rigorous preparatory bridge for Drama 4, making it an ideal choice for students looking to refine their craft before entering the advanced production level. Students will move beyond simple performance into the mechanics of playwriting, improvisation, and the study of Shakespeare, while continuing to sharpen their acting techniques and historical knowledge. This course emphasizes the collaborative nature of the arts; students will participate as both dedicated performers and engaged audience members, working together to bring various in-class performance opportunities to life. Whether you are building your portfolio for next year or looking to expand your creative range, this class provides the tools to master the stage.

Students will participate in the Shakespeare Competition and they will compete in the UHSAA Drama competition. Students are expected to maintain a standard of excellence, being a good example for the rest of the department.

description: This is an English course, and follows the core curriculum. The specific focus of the class is on the structure and form of visual text (or film). Students will watch, discuss, evaluate, and analyze several short and full-length films with an emphasis on understanding how film functions as a form of literature. Students will learn literary evaluation techniques, critical reading skills, and critical analysis techniques including the use of text-appropriate vocabulary and several theories. Additionally, students will write literary analysis and evaluation essays, concepts, treatments, screenplays, and shooting scripts.

Humanities studies the fundamentally human need to share our experience through creative artistic expression--to be citizens of a community beyond ourselves. We'll understand and enjoy various art forms (visual arts, performing arts, architecture, and literature) by thinking critically about their formal compositional elements, historical context, and principles of creative production. We’ll also apply these creative principles to how we create everyday, from athletics to everyday fashion and design. We’ll become creators ourselves. Perhaps most importantly, we'll understand that collaboratively sharing creative expression is a powerful way to improve our own character by increasing empathy for and connection to others.

This is an English 12 or Fine Arts elective course, and students may choose which .50 credit they would like to earn at registration. Music Language Arts explores the power of rhetoric combined with music theory in popular culture throughout history—from Catholic composers working surreptitiously in the courts of Anglican kings to the record-breaking artistry of Taylor Swift; from heartbroken cowboys to Vietnam protests—Civil War songs to African American Spirituals. Sung music provides a detailed record of culture, values, and zeitgeist, which we will analyze for its artistic merit, historical significance, and use of persuasive, argumentative, and expressive language. We will study the music elements of rhythm, harmonic progression, meter, and melodic composition while exploring the rhetorical situation, rhetorical devices, figurative language, and rhetorical appeals as the universal vehicles for carrying language across geographic borders, cultures, peoples, and time.

1st Semester Concurrent Enrollment: UVU MATH 1010 (Intermediate Algebra) (4 semester hrs UVU credit) This course expands and covers more in depth basic algebra concepts introduced in Beginning Algebra. Topics of study include linear and quadratic equations and inequalities, polynomials and rational expressions, radical and exponential expressions and equations, complex numbers, systems of linear and nonlinear equations, functions, conic sections, and real world applications of algebra. *NOTE: MATH 1010 will not satisfy math course requirements for the Regents’ Scholarship, unless MATH 1050 is taken semester 2.

2nd Semester Concurrent Enrollment: UVU MATH 1050 College Algebra - QL) (4 semester hrs UVU credit) Course includes inequalities, functions and their graphs, polynomial and rational functions, exponential and logarithmic functions, systems of linear and nonlinear equations, matrices and determinants, arithmetic and geometric sequences, and the Binomial Theorem.

MATH DECISION MAKING is designed to help students who are not currently planning to study Math, Science, and Engineering in college. This course should also be considered by students who will benefit from a review of the core concepts they learned in Secondary Math II and additional instruction for how to use math in more applied settings. The course goal is to use critical thinking to apply students' math skills and abilities in real-life scenarios they will encounter now and after graduation. Students will use statistics, logic, probability, mathematical modeling, and financial management concepts to make decisions, create estimates and forecasts, and set goals for their lives to accomplish the course goals. These concepts include investing for future financial needs, buying insurance, evaluating probabilities, and making reasoned judgments about the world around them. The class covers an entire school year; however, the four quarters of instruction are independent of each other, allowing students to enter and exit the course quarterly as needed. DATA SCIENCE (A/AME) Course Length: Full year This course will develop skills in Data Science and statistics by emphasizing a modeling approach. Students will reason and make sense of data using the Statistical Problem Solving Process, consisting of: 1) Formulating Statistical Investigative Questions, 2) Collect/Consider Data, 3) Analyze Data, and 4) Interpreting the Results. Students will learn to use data and modeling to formulate and answer questions as well as to critically evaluate information using mathematics as the reasoning tool. The class will use the R computer coding language and Jupyter notebooks - two tools used by actual data scientists in the real world. This course is an equivalent replacement for Secondary Math 3. Students could take AP Statistics or Math 1010 after this course.

Students assist in the operation of the Media Center. It is important that students be able to follow procedures, have some self-motivation, and be willing to do the tasks necessary to assist the media specialist.

*UVU credit may be earned via the Ed. Net class only This full year course provides students with an in depth study of health care careers, including actual clinical experience in therapeutic, diagnostic, information, and environmental health care settings. Instruction includes intermediate anatomy and physiology, medical terminology, diseases and disorders, medical ethics, and first aid. The class is designed to prepare students for the advanced health science course and/or for a variety of health technology programs.

Anyone interested in a career associated with the health industry will gain valuable experience to prepare them for college classes. Instruction for this full year class includes intermediate anatomy and physiology, careers associated with the health industry, medical terminology and diseases and disorders. Even if you are not interested in a health career you will gain vital information on how to care for your body.

*UVU CE Credit may be earned This is a one-semester course that is intended for students who are interested in pursuing a career in health care. It is also for students who desire to heighten their ability to communicate and understand medical jargon. This course stresses terminology usage and accuracy. We will study the elements, abbreviations, spelling, pronunciation, and logic of medical terminology. Some understanding of general anatomy will be taught as well. Instruction includes lectures, audiovisual presentations, and hands-on activities. INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY EDUCATION

Concurrent Enrollment Credit: METO 1010 (UVU) This is a Concurrent Enrollment Course, offering both high school credit through Timpanogos High School and college credit through Utah Valley University. Credit from this course is transferable to all colleges and universities. Introduces the study of our atmosphere. Studies the Earth's dynamic weather systems. Covers structure and compositions of the atmosphere; weather patterns; air masses; and types of weather fronts, weather forecasting, and climates.
Course description unavailable.

Audition Required This class is designed for students who are serious about performance, and specifically about musical theatre. This class has a high focus on how to improve acting skills within musical theatre performance. Students in this class have been selected for both their singing, acting, and dancing abilities, and also for their willingness to commit to the THS drama department. Students are expected to maintain a standard of excellence, being a good example for the rest of the department. Students will explore various acting methods, singing techniques, dance styles, musical theatre history, and more! All of those areas of study will be focused on as they rehearse and perform in a THS Musical Theatre Showcase. Students will participate as performers and audience members all in an effort to help them become more confident, empathetic, understanding and cooperative.

An instructional course that explores conservation and management of natural resources. The course includes instruction in natural resource science, ecology, population and habitat management, soil and water systems, air quality, rangeland management, waste management, and outdoor recreation safety. Students study local, state, and federal regulations governing natural resource use; evaluate ethical and sustainability issues; analyze population and environmental data; and explore career pathways in natural resource science. SOCIAL STUDIES

*UVU credit may be earned This course covers the fundamentals of networking, such as network hardware and configuration, the use of protocols to enable reliable and accurate transmission of data between different hosts around the world, and relevant security practices that secure the transmission of data both within and between computer networks.
Students assist in the operation of the school offices (Main, Counseling, Attendance)

This is an audition orchestra, with registration by instructor permission only. Individuals who played with this orchestra in a previous year at THS are typically approved to register in subsequent years without further audition. Chamber Orchestra is reserved for students playing at a medium-advanced to an advanced level, who wish to learn and perform a wide range of chamber music at a high level of excellence. Members are self-motivated and committed to consistent individual practice outside of class rehearsal time. While not required, private instruction is recommended. The chamber orchestra typically performs once each term, but also performs at Region and potentially State Orchestra festivals. A commitment to participate in every performance is required. Students also participate in the THS Instrumental Solo & Ensemble Festival, with an opportunity to qualify for both Region and State Festivals. This orchestra may visit local junior high schools for recruiting and go on a Musicianship tour during the year – with participation by members of the ensemble expected. Members of this ensemble have the opportunity to perform as a full symphony orchestra and also with the THS Choirs. Attendance at rehearsals and performances both before and after school will be required frequently.

Application Required Fees required for summer workshop and uniform. This course focuses on service and on the qualities of leadership. Students work to develop these qualities in the lives of students and participate in and lead service projects and activities. The PACK also teams with Windsor Elementary School in Kid’s Cause to meet the needs of Windsor’s families.

This course is for students who already have some understanding of drawing, but want to extend their artistic experiences into painting. This course builds and strengthens foundations of color, composition, and diverse painting techniques, such as watercolor, acrylic, oil, etc. Students learn painting basics like brush control, color mixing and blending along with exposure to different culture’s art methods. This class is available to students at all skill levels, whether beginning in art or already enrolled in AP Art.

This course furthers your painting skills with a lot more freedom than Painting 1. The emphasis is on painting animals, still lifes, landscapes and portraits with more exploration in acrylics, oils and watercolor. Additional painting media will be introduced like gouache, digital painting, etc.

Peer tutors will get to know their peers with intellectual and physical disabilities and assist them in their Life Skills (special education) and/or mainstream courses. This class is an opportunity to help students gain essential skills for life in an environment where you are truly needed and appreciated. You will also have the opportunity to make friends with some of the most amazing people you will ever meet.

This is a one semester course designed to offer opportunities to participate in a wide variety of physical activities with students in the Life Skills class. The curriculum emphasizes teamwork and team building rather than competitiveness. Some of the activities taught in this class are: basketball, volleyball, curing, snowshoeing, weight lifting and yoga. These activities coincide with Unified PE tournaments throughout the year. The curriculum provides diverse activities to meet the individual needs of all students and develops competency in several sports and lifetime activities.

In this course students will learn about the physical world around them in much the same way as scientists do. Among topics to be covered are motion, forces, energy, fields, and waves. Students will be confronted with a series of situations in which they will decide what factors might be related, how to measure them, and then collect data. Students will use computers to help analyze the data, and learn how to clearly describe the relationships, using a variety of graphical and mathematical representations. Students will then go on to explore how these relationships can be applied to new situations.

sourceTitles: PRINCIPLES OF CYBER DEFENSE CE C64020 UIT170 (CTE)
description: *UVU credit may be earned In this course, students will explore the current cyber threat landscape to understand the types of adversaries organizations face and the techniques adversaries use to compromise systems and data, how vulnerabilities create risks, and how organizations implement security controls to manage those risks. Students will research emerging trends in cybersecurity and gain hands-on experience implementing security protocols.

This course introduces the student to the study of the behavior of individuals and why people act the way that they do. It emphasizes the manner in which the individual can apply various psychological theories and concepts to better understand oneself, ones motives, and ones relationship with other people.
A student may be released to attend off-campus religious instructions.

Retailing is a class that will prepare the student to operate businesses that sell, rent, or lease goods and services. This course will provide activity-oriented training in buying, storing, pricing, advertising, displaying, financing, and other activities necessary for successful business operations. This class runs Titan’s Den (School store during lunchtime and breakfast) which will give you the opportunity to gain retail experience and provide a valuable service to the school community.

This is an entry level course that introduces basic methods for making 3-D art. Mediums such as cardboard, tape, plaster and clay will be used in production. Students will be exposed to many different ways to create art. Great class if you do not like painting or drawing but just making “stuff”.

Prior Course Progression Intermediate Math 2 THE FUNDAMENTAL PURPOSE OF SECONDARY MATHEMATICS I is to formalize and extend the mathematics that students learned in the middle grades. The critical areas, organized into units, deepen and extend understanding of linear relationships, in part by contrasting them with exponential phenomena, and in part by applying linear models to data that exhibit a linear trend. Secondary Mathematics I uses properties and theorems involving congruent figures to deepen and extend understanding of geometric knowledge from prior grades. The final unit in the course ties together the algebraic and geometric ideas studied. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations.

Prior Course Progression Secondary Math 1 THE FOCUS OF SECONDARY MATHEMATICS II is on quadratic expressions, equations, and functions and on comparing their characteristics and behavior to those of linear and exponential relationships from Secondary Mathematics I as organized into six critical areas, or units. The need for extending the set of rational numbers arises, and real and complex numbers are introduced so that all quadratic equations can be solved. The link between probability and data is explored through conditional probability and counting methods, including their use in making and evaluating decisions. The study of similarity leads to an understanding of right triangle trigonometry and connects to quadratics through Pythagorean relationships. Circles, with their quadratic algebraic representations, round out the course. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. Both options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math II Extended Secondary Math II Regular

Prior Course Progression Secondary Math II Grades IN SECONDARY MATHEMATICS III students pull together and apply the accumulation of learning that they have from their previous courses, with content grouped into four critical areas, organized into units. They apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. And, finally, students bring together all of their experience with functions and geometry to create models and solve contextual problems. The Mathematical Practice Standards apply throughout each course and, together with the content standards, prescribe that students experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations. Two options are available in this course (Extended & Regular). A student should select the option that their current teacher recommends. All three options will cover the content described above, but differ in the depth of coverage and extensions included. Secondary Math III Extended Secondary Math III Extended covers the same algebraic and mathematical reasoning concepts as Secondary Math III Regular, but dives deeper into function analysis, trig identities, parametric equations, and polar coordinates. It combines Secondary Math III with precalculus content into a single course and is designed for students planning to take AP Calculus (AB or BC) and AP Statistics in their junior and/or senior years. Secondary Math III Regular Secondary Math III will expand students' understanding of algebraic and mathematical reasoning. The course covers polynomial, rational, and exponential functions, trigonometry, statistics, and an introduction to pre-calculus concepts. Students develop skills in modeling real-world situations and interpreting data. Secondary Math III prepares students for college-level mathematics offered at Timpanogos High School, including AP Statistics, College Algebra (concurrent enrollment), and AP Calculus (AB).

sourceTitles: Sewing II (APPAREL DESIGN AND PRODUCTION II) (CTE)
description: Sewing 2 is an intermediate sewing class for students who have already taken Sports Sewing or Sewing 1. Students will further strengthen and broaden apparel design and production techniques. In this course students will construct a hoodie, zipper projects, hand embroidery, basic alterations and other intermediate projects. These skills prepare students for the exciting global apparel industry and entrepreneurial opportunities.

This course introduces the student to the principles of sociology. Sociology is the study of people! Students will study society with the goal of learning to critically think like a sociologist and learn to better embrace, accept, and respect themselves and others’ differences. Topics and ideas covered include culture, socialization, social inequalities, crime and deviance, and social work in the real world.

Teacher Approval Fees required for summer workshop and uniform. $50+ Sources of Strength is a leadership coalition at Timpanogos High School that works to change unhealthy norms and culture, ultimately preventing suicide, bullying, and substance abuse. The mission of Sources of Strength is to prevent unhealthy activities by increasing help seeking behaviors and promoting connections between peers and caring adults. Sources of Strength teaches students to cultivate positive aspects of a person's life like family support, positive friends, mentors, healthy activities, generosity, spirituality, physical health, and mental health. Sources of Strength involves peer leadership and positive messaging campaigns to spread awareness within our school community.

$5 Fee required Students begin to communicate sufficiently to satisfy basic survival needs and minimum courtesy requirements. They can ask and answer some simple questions, respond to and sometimes initiate short statements and maintain simple conversation. Students understand and interpret written and spoken language on a variety of topics. Students will begin to identify and react to perspectives and practices of the Spanish culture.

$5 Fee required Students develop further communication skills and are able to satisfy most survival needs and limited social demands. They can initiate and sustain a short conversation on factual topics. They can give autobiographical information and discuss leisure time activities. Students are able to present information and ideas to an audience. They can describe characteristics and behaviors of everyday life in the Spanish culture and they can identify the expressive forms of the culture, such as art, literature, music and dance.

$5 Fee required The goal of this course is to continue proficiency of key language skills in Spanish. Continuing emphasis will be on speaking and listening comprehension with increasing development of reading and writing skills. Communicative activities will form the basis of classroom instruction; active participation in the target language and constant attendance is essential. Class instruction will be given in Spanish.

$5 Fee required The goal of this course is to increase and enhance proficiency of key language skills in Spanish. Sports Sewing is a beginners sewing class, designed to introduce students to basic sewing skills. Students will learn about the use and care of the sewing machine while sewing a: lanyard, drawstring backpack, pin cushion, and pajama pants. Learn about fabrics, pattern selections, consumer skills and problem solving.

Sports History concentrates largely on the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. In addition to examining the history of different sports, particularly those thought of as “national” sports, the course will also explore the Olympics, the rise of the athlete as a popular cultural hero, issues of gender and race in the sporting world, the impact of sports on social mobility, and the extent to which debates within the U.S. sporting world have changed over time.

This course is designed for students who are interested in the technical and design aspects of theater. They will learn about all areas involved behind the stage in the THS drama department: lights, sound, sets, costumes, props and stage management. There will not be extensive homework in this class, but there is a 30-hour outside of class commitment for crew work.

Application Required This course is designed for students who want to specialize in the technical and design aspects of theater. It is for students who have taken stagecraft previously, or who excel in technical design. By application only, students will be on our tech crew. This crew is in charge of covering events and rentals at the school. They help run assemblies and are relied on heavily for our mainstage productions. These students also play a large role in the design aspects of the plays and musicals at the school. They will learn about all areas involved behind the stage in the THS drama department: lights, sound, sets, costumes, props and stage management, but in a more advanced capacity. Stage crew is paid for their work on events outside of school hours. MUSIC

This is one of the only high school stained glass classes taught in the country! Come learn how to cut glass and solder it into a beautiful art piece. There is a brief exposure to lead but precautions are taken to keep students safe. You will be making 4-5 stained glass pieces including a small window panel and a 3-D glass project. Anybody can do this so do not worry if you have no previous art abilities.

We will be continuing learning stained glass techniques including painting glass and working on the school stained glass mural. Students will have the opportunity to make larger pieces and have more freedom to make what they wish in glass. Lead is used briefly in the solder process but precautions are taken to keep students safe. ART-PERFORMING

Holocaust education requires a comprehensive study of not only times, dates, and places, but also the motivation and ideology that allowed these events. In this course, students will study the history of anti-Semitism; the rise of the Nazi party; and the Holocaust, from its beginnings through liberation and the aftermath of the tragedy. The study of the Holocaust is a multi-disciplinary one, integrating world history, geography, and civics. Through this in-depth, semester-long study of the Holocaust, high school students will gain an understanding of the ramifications of prejudice and indifference, the potential for government- supported terror, and they will get glimpses of kindness and humanity in the worst of times.

Students will use the THS website and social media channels to disperse information to our students, faculty, and community. Some after-school work may be required.

This course emphasizes the history of the United States, through the study of basic geographic, economic, social, political, and historical developments following reconstruction and the westward movement. Particular emphasis is placed on the Constitution, the American system of government, law related/citizenship education. Students will identify ways to solve problems, make decisions, and participate as a responsible citizen of the United States, while developing basic tools of historical interpretation, research, and analysis.

This course is designed to provide students with the basic knowledge and skills related to the television broadcasting industry and video production. Includes instruction and hands-on assignments in the following areas: camera operation, audio systems, lighting systems, pre-production, studio operations, control room operations, visual effects and graphics, and copyright laws. Student projects include commercials, psa’s, news stories, music videos, short films, documentaries, and special effects assignments.

Students will learn the basics of web design and development, which includes the foundations of HTML and CSS. code markup, design concepts and web graphics manipulation, page layout, form development, and usability and accessibility issues. Students will learn and practice knowledge, attitude, skills, and habits required for performing tasks autonomously, including the selection and use of appropriate techniques and equipment with minimum supervision.

This is an intermediate level course that will teach the next level of welding skills. This course will prepare students to apply technical knowledge and skill in the workplace and in project construction. In this course, students will learn and practice knowledge, attitude, skills, and habits required for performing tasks autonomously, including the selection and use of appropriate techniques and equipment with minimum supervision.

This is an advanced welding skills course. This course will teach students the processes that will prepare them to apply technical knowledge and skill in the workplace and in project construction. This class is a one semester course and has been designed to introduce students to the basic principles and safety techniques utilized in weight training. The overall purpose of this class is to demonstrate, through class participation, how the use of weight training can be effective in helping to develop a healthy lifestyle. The curriculum meets the needs of individual students and emphasizes physical fitness and proper nutrition. This class fills elective credit needed for graduation.

World Religions is a comparative course designed to help students gain a better understanding of, and an appreciation for, a variety of religious beliefs, traditions, and practices around the world, thereby helping students develop a general sense of religious tolerance and respect. The course will compare and contrast the major religions of the world, including Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, and Judaism. We will also look into a variety of lesser known religious beliefs and practices.
Course description unavailable.

Year Do you like photography, design, journalism or social networking? If so, the THS yearbook staff wants you! Students have fun making friends while producing a book of unforgettable memories for their classmates. This year-long course provides skill development in the electronic procedures of producing and editing publications. Students will create, format, illustrate, design, edit/revise, and print publications. This year will never happen again! Yearbook it! Students are chosen for the yearbook staff based on applications and teacher recommendations. See Kris Caldwell-Advisor for applications and information.

Fall or Spring Semester Yoga 101 is designed to introduce students to the fundamentals of yoga practice, including various poses, breathing techniques, and mindfulness exercises. This course aims to improve flexibility, strength, and mental well-being while providing students with tools to manage stress and promote relaxation. Course curriculum will focus on learning foundational poses and vinyasas, proper alignment, and basic breathing and meditation practices. Instruction will cater to different fitness levels and abilities, allowing modifications for individual needs.

This class will survey the biodiversity of animals and their evolutionary relationships. Emphasis will be placed on natural history and comparative anatomy. The class will emphasize science communication and the nature of science as important themes and research projects will include both written and oral presentations. Course requirements will also include mandatory dissections and a species list which will require students to spend time outside observing animals.
